summary: Graham argues that while recessions typically reduce VC funding and startup formation, this time startups may keep forming because the cost to build and reach profitability has fallen dramatically. That decoupling means founders can become profitable with minimal funding, so a pullback in VC could cause founders to move on without them, potentially making VCs less relevant when the economy recovers.
key_points: Startup costs have dropped due to cheap hardware, free software, free distribution, and more productive languages.
Even small revenue can create infinite runway and reduce reliance on VC.
Founder interest in starting startups remains strong despite the recession.
Some YC startups prefer avoiding investor time costs if they can reach profitability.
If founders normalize not taking VC, the whole VC industry could lose.
money_quotes: Once you're profitable you don't need investors' money.
For many startups, VC funding has, in the language of VCs, gone from a must-have to a nice-to-have.
VCs and founders are like two components that used to be bolted together.
If you lose a deal to None, all VCs lose.
This time founders may keep starting startups.
key_insight: Cheaper startup costs make profitability attainable without VC, so a recession could permanently decouple founders from investors and erode VC relevance.
